ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Filipino-style fried noodles with prawns; 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dishes — stir-fried noodle dish with mixed ingredients
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific Filipino pancit code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Filipino-style fried noodles; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Filipino-style fried noodles with prawns weighs approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-person restaurant or hawker serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), cooked / Noodles, egg, cooked, enriched; adapted composite for Filipino-style stir-fried noodle dish with prawns and vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Filipino pancit guisado as a composite dish. Values are derived from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 172015), cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), and stir-fried mixed vegetables. The input per_100g values are within plausible ranges for this dish type and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient data for this composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. — Noodles, instant, boiled (GI ~58); used as analogous estimate for stir-fried egg/rice noodle dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Filipino pancit guisado. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled/stir-fried Asian noodles (egg noodles and rice vermicelli, GI range 53–65, midpoint ~58). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 70.0) / 100 = 40.6, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 17.5 × 4 = 70.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 375 mg per 100 g from multiple high-sodium condiments (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce); a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,500 mg sodium, representing 65–75% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, request reduced-sodium soy sauce and minimal fish sauce, and increase the vegetable proportion to partially offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined noodle base (egg noodles or rice vermicelli) with a medium-high GI of approximately 58 and low dietary fibre of 1.25 g per 100 g results in rapid postprandial glucose absorption; a 400 g serving delivers 70 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 40.6.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 150–200 g, request wholemeal or higher-fibre noodle alternatives where available, and increase the vegetable-to-noodle ratio to slow glucose absorption and reduce the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is 2.0 g per 100 g and sodium is 375 mg per 100 g; trans fat is present at 0.05 g per 100 g from cooking oil; cholesterol is 25 mg per 100 g from prawns; the combination of elevated sodium and saturated fat is unfavourable for cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request preparation with minimal oil and reduced-sodium condiments, and choose dishes with a higher proportion of vegetables and lean protein to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium from multiple condiments may promote tissue swelling and inflammation; refined noodle base with low fibre contributes to glycaemic-driven inflammation; nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should reduce fish sauce and soy sauce quantities, monitor tolerance to nightshade vegetables, and consider adding anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or turmeric to the stir-fry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Filipino-Style Fried Noodles with Prawns (Pancit Guisado) is a classic Southeast Asian stir-fried noodle dish prepared in the Filipino tradition. The dish consists of egg noodles or rice vermicelli wok-tossed at high heat with juicy prawns, shredded cabbage, julienned carrots, bell peppers, garlic, and onion, seasoned with a combination of soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce. The result is a savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-rich dish with a tender-chewy noodle texture and a fragrant wok-charred aroma. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 6.25 g protein, 7.5 g fat (2.0 g saturated), 17.5 g carbohydrate, 1.25 g dietary fibre, and 375 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 58 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 40.6 per standard 400 g plate. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to its sodium content from multiple condiments, high concern for type 2 diabetes due to its refined noodle base and high glycemic load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to combined sodium and saturated fat. The dish is not suitable for individuals on low-sodium diets without significant modification. It is a popular hawker and restaurant item in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, commonly served as a main meal at lunch or dinner. Allergens include shellfish (prawns), gluten (wheat noodles), soy, fish, and egg.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Filipino fried noodles?
Is pancit guisado suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of fried noodles with prawns
Can hypertensive patients eat fried noodles?
What is the glycemic index of stir-fried noodles?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central) contains an exact entry for Filipino-style fried noodles with prawns as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked egg noodles, cooked shrimp, and stir-fried vegetables. Values are within plausible ranges for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, B-vitamin fractions, and moisture data for this composite Filipino-style stir-fried noodle dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 2. The input record listed two identical portion entries ('1 plate', 400 g); the duplicate has been collapsed to a single serving entry. 3. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database and is a forbidden source for primary nutrient evidence; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved adapted source. 4. The dish is described as Filipino-style in the input; country_of_origin is listed as Singapore in the input, reflecting the serving context (Singapore hawker/restaurant), while cuisine origin is correctly attributed to the Philippines. 5. Halal status is marked false because fish sauce (patis) and oyster sauce may contain non-halal processing agents depending on the specific brand; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 6. Trans fat of 0.05 g per 100 g is retained from the input; this is a trace level consistent with cooking oil usage and is not confirmed zero — it is a plausible trace value. 7. GI estimated at 58 (medium) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for boiled/stir-fried Asian noodles; glycemic load per 400 g serving = 40.6 (high).
